title: Rustic ciabatta
subtitle: Wet-dough Italian bread with a large, irregular crumb
excerpt: Ciabatta is a high-hydration Italian bread with a thin, floury crust and a wide-open, chewy crumb full of irregular holes. The wet dough is more batter than dough and is handled very gently throughout to preserve the bubbles. Fold rather than knead; rest rather than shape.
sourceNotes: Ciabatta was developed in northern Italy in the 1980s, initially in the Veneto. The formula (high-hydration straight dough, stretched and folded rather than kneaded) is well documented in Italian baking literature. The version here is a home adaptation of the standard ciabatta formula using commercial yeast.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced or in halves, wrapped tightly. Refresh from frozen in a hot oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix the dough the night before and retard in the fridge overnight. The slow ferment improves flavour; bring to room temperature for 1 hour before dividing and baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake directly on a preheated tray or stone
baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 8 minutes

content.text: Ciabatta is a very wet dough (80% hydration, almost pourable) handled gently to preserve the large air pockets that give the bread its open crumb. There is no shaping in the conventional sense; the loaves are poured and stretched from the bowl rather than formed by hand. The reward is a light, airy interior under a thin, flour-dusted crust.
Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer takes the guessing out, particularly at the high temperatures ciabatta needs.

Stir the flour, yeast, and salt together in a large bowl. Add the water and olive oil and mix thoroughly with a spoon or your hand until no dry flour remains. The mixture is wet and sticky, more batter than dough. Cover the bowl.
Stretch and fold, four times over 2 hours

Over the next 2 hours, stretch and fold the dough four times at 30-minute intervals. To fold: wet your hand, reach under one side of the dough, stretch it up, and fold it over the top. Rotate the bowl and repeat from all four sides. Do not stir or knead; the folding builds structure without degassing. Between folds, the dough should become progressively smoother and more elastic. By the fourth fold it should hold its shape briefly when you lift the edge.
After the final fold, leave the dough covered for a further 30 minutes. It should look airy and show visible bubbles under the surface.

Heat the oven to 230°C fan with two baking trays inside. Pour the dough onto a generously floured surface. Dust the top with flour and use a bench scraper to divide it in half. Ease each half onto a peel or floured board, stretching it gently into a rectangle. Slide onto the hot baking trays, flour-side up. Bake with steam for 22 to 25 minutes until deep golden, hollow-sounding, and blistered. Cool on a wire rack for 20 minutes before slicing.
